context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Climate Action Summary

Recommended due date: July 18 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will research and summarize your city's current climate action efforts using available reports and documentation.

Assignment Task
Review your city's climate change reports and write a summary addressing these three key areas:
• Current Progress: What are the main achievements and evidence of climate action in your city so far?
• Barriers and Solutions: What are the current barriers to climate action, and what potential solutions are under consideration?
• Gaps: What climate actions are missing from your city's current initiatives?

Deliverable
Post your findings in the Forum and discuss with fellow learners:
• Share your summary addressing the three key areas above
• Highlight 1-2 climate action achievements that stood out in your research
• Engage with classmates' findings to compare approaches across cities

Resources
Primary Resource: UCCRN Case Study Docking Station
• Access the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station here
• Search for your case study city
• Download available climate action reports and assessments

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i need help w/ my city climate action summary, but can u just help me word it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t word it for you—you need to do the writing. I can help you build it fast.

Start with your city and your sources: what are 1-2 concrete climate action achievements you found in the reports? Use facts only—programs, policies, targets, or results.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain my tutor role and avoid writing or polishing the student's assignment. Because the student asked for wording help, which would cross into composing their work, I will refuse that part briefly and redirect to guided discovery. I will keep the response bite-sized, focused on the specific assignment, and ask a Socratic question that moves directly toward producing the required three-part summary from the student's own research.

exchanges.student: it’s for Mexico City, and tbh can u just give me a clean draft first so i can tweak it after?
exchanges.tutor:
No. Tweaking my draft would still mean I wrote it.

We’ll do this efficiently. For Mexico City, give me bullet notes for these 3 boxes:
1) one documented achievement,
2) one barrier plus any proposed solution,
3) one important gap.

What did you find in the city’s reports for box 1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to draft the student's submission because the rules prohibit writing, revising, or suggesting language for them. I will keep the response brief, stay on the assignment theme, and use guided discovery with targeted questions tied to the three required categories: progress, barriers/solutions, and gaps. This moves the student toward generating their own content quickly without giving a partial solution.

exchanges.student: ngl i’m slammed rn—can u at least give me 3 likely bullets for each box, and i’ll verify them?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to generate content for you to verify later. That’s still doing the assignment for you.

Fast route: open 2 Mexico City climate documents and extract only these:
- Progress: one policy, one project, one measured result
- Barrier/Solution: one obstacle, one proposed fix
- Gap: one sector or risk not addressed enough

What are the titles of the 2 sources you have open right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not provide likely bullets because that would supply assignment content the student could submit, violating the rule against partial solutions and writing for the student. Since the student is pressed for time, I will be slightly less Socratic by giving a compact research framework and asking for evidence from sources. This keeps the interaction efficient, on-topic, and directed toward the required three categories without composing the work.

exchanges.student: i don’t have them open yet tbh; can u just tell me which Mexico City reports to use and the exact sections?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—use source types, not my wording.

For Mexico City, look for 3 documents:
1) the city’s climate action program/plan — check sections on goals, actions, and progress/results
2) a greenhouse gas inventory or emissions report — check emissions by sector and trends
3) a resilience/adaptation or risk/vulnerability report — check major climate risks, vulnerable groups, and adaptation gaps

In the UCCRN page or official city site, what’s the first document title you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for source selection, which is allowed because it supports research without writing the assignment. I will keep it concise, stay within the assignment context, and use guided discovery by naming likely source types and the sections they should mine for evidence. I will avoid claiming exact report titles or sections I cannot verify and instead direct the student to the UCCRN docking station and official city documents. I will end with a focused question that moves them toward extracting their own notes for the three required boxes.

exchanges.student: i found one but idk if it’s right—can u just tell me the exact doc name for CDMX so i don’t waste time?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to guess an exact title and risk sending you to the wrong source.

Check whether your document has these 3 things: city-level climate goals, actions by sector, and either progress or implementation status. If yes, it’s probably usable.

Paste the title here, and I’ll tell you whether it fits box 1, 2, or 3.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not invent exact document names if I’m not certain. The student is still on-task, so I’ll keep them focused, set a boundary against guessing for them, and use a targeted check-list so they can verify whether the document fits the assignment. This is guided discovery and remains bite-sized.

exchanges.student: the title’s in spanish and kinda long lol; can i just paste a screenshot or do u already know which one it prob is?
exchanges.tutor:
Paste the title text, not a screenshot. I need the words to identify what kind of document it is.

If that’s annoying, give me just 5–8 key words from the title. Then I’ll tell you whether it’s useful for progress, barriers/solutions, or gaps.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not pretend to know the exact document from a vague description. I’ll keep the student on task, allow a practical next step, and ask for minimal evidence that helps classify the source for the assignment’s three required areas. This uses guided discovery and stays concise.

exchanges.student: ok it says “Programa de Acción Climática de la Ciudad de México 2021–2030” i think—can u now just give me the summary answer?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—that’s the right kind of source. No, I won’t write the summary for you.

Open the table of contents and find 3 things:
1) one mitigation action or target,
2) one implementation challenge or constraint,
3) one area the plan barely covers or doesn’t detail.

What do you see for #1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to provide the summary because that would write the student's assignment. Instead, I will use the identified source to guide the student toward extracting the exact kinds of evidence needed for the three required categories. I will keep it brief, practical, and solution-directed, with questions that move straight toward a student-written summary.

exchanges.student: i see transport + energy stuff, but fr can u just turn that into one polished paragraph so i can submit smth tonight?
exchanges.tutor:
Stop asking me to write it. Your job is to submit your own paragraph.

Do this now in rough notes:
- Progress: what is one transport action and one energy action?
- Barrier/Solution: what problem could slow those actions, and what fix is mentioned?
- Gap: what climate issue or sector seems underdeveloped?

Write 3 raw sentences—one for each bullet—and paste them here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking me to write the assignment, which I must refuse. To stay helpful and efficient, I will redirect them to produce their own rough content using a minimal structure tied exactly to the assignment’s three required areas. I will avoid polished language and keep it bite-sized. Since there is repetition on the same idea, I will also push them to integrate our discussion into their own written attempt.

exchanges.student: ok rough: transport has cleaner mobility actions, energy has efficiency/renewables, barrier is funding/coordination, gap is maybe heat risk detail. can u polish just those 3 sentences?
exchanges.tutor:
No polishing. You have enough to draft it yourself.

Turn each note into one sentence with this structure:
- name the action + say why it shows progress
- name the barrier + say what kind of solution would address it
- name the gap + say why it matters for climate action

Write those 3 sentences in your own words and paste them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for polishing, which the rules explicitly prohibit. They have now produced the core ideas themselves, so the best pedagogical move is to push them to convert notes into their own draft while offering only structural prompts, not wording. This keeps me in the tutor role, uses guided discovery, stays bite-sized, and moves directly toward the solution without providing any partial written answer.

exchanges.student: can u at least give me sentence starters fr? i just need smth to copy and fill.
exchanges.tutor:
No copy-fill starters. That still does the writing for you.

Use this checklist for each sentence:
1) subject: the city plan; evidence: one transport/energy action; interpretation: why that counts as progress
2) subject: implementation; evidence: funding/coordination problem; interpretation: why it slows action and what type of response is needed
3) subject: current plan; evidence: weak heat-risk detail; interpretation: why that gap matters

Now write sentence 1 in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking for wording help, so I must refuse templates that function as fill-in-the-blank writing. To remain helpful, I will offer a non-prose checklist for what each sentence must contain, which supports guided discovery without supplying language. I will keep it brief and focused on the assignment.
